As for your work and hobbies... oh, I could talk about that for a long time, listen to you for hours. Please tell me as soon as you can, I'm all ears.

I was born in London (Chelsea) and went to St Peter’s (Eaton Sq) infant school for 2 years until I was 6 years old. [intimate message omitted]. So I did junior school and high school there.

At junior school, I was one of those INCREDIBLY annoying seven-year-olds who was always the first to put his hand up to answer the teacher’s questions… every time… You know the type… “Miss, miss, I know, I know… choose me… choose me…” It reached the point where Miss Whatever started to ignore my pleas. “Gavin, put your hand down Let someone else answer…” I thought about saying, “But Miss, they don’t know the answers, they’re a bunch of thickies…” I decided against. I may have been annoying, [intimate message omitted].

Anyway…

“What is the opposite of Imports?” “Miss, Miss etc…” “Exports, Miss”

“Now, if you were a glider pilot, where would head for to find an updraught?” There are a lot of smart-aleck answers to that question, but I wasn’t (yet) a smart-aleck, I was merely incredibly annoying.

“Miss, Miss etc…” “Put your hand down, Gavin…” “Anyone…?”

“But, Miss, Miss etc…” This went on for a few minutes…

“Gavin…?” “Underneath a cloud, Miss.” There was a brief, stunned silence from Miss. (How did a seven-year-old know that?)

High school was certainly a step up. But in a good high school it was meant to be just that. This one was good. I had to take an exam to get in.

Victor Slade (Vic Slade) was our first year English master, and looked the part. Ginger hair, ginger beard, red tie. Very early on he taught us about Haiku, the Japanese verse form, comprising just 17 syllables,

(Yup, 17. That’s all you get.) frequently in a 5-7-5 format. With about 20 minutes to go, he said,

“Right! I want you to compose a haiku for me. About anything.”

“What?” We thought. [intimate message omitted]. “Mr Chart,” he said. “Have you finished?” “Yes, Sir,” I replied.

He walked back to his desk with my paper and sat down to read it.

There was a loud snort and a chuckle. I had written this:

My Haiku

“To express oneself

In seventeen syllables

Is very diffic---”

[Gavin Chart, aged 11]

When I was 13, I wanted to be a writer. Our English teacher, Mr Tett, [intimate message omitted] This was catnip to a cat. It didn’t count towards any school grades, he just wanted to see what we would do if given a free-hand. Quite arbitrarily, he said that it was worth 100 (points).

I remember the day I handed in my “novel” and walking back to my desk. I didn’t see it again for six weeks. He apologized to us, afterwards, for the delay, but thought our efforts were so good that he handed them round to all the other teachers in the school to read (with all THEIR families).

He handed them back to us, one at a time. He said nothing to me. But inside was a note, “99/100. Not bad. Echoes of Tolkien?” (He knew I disliked Tolkien intensely. He was always winding me up.) I loved those English teachers, Vic Slade, Neil Tett et al. They always pushed you and waited to see how you would react. I thought much later what great training that was for the grown-up world.

To be continued

As I wanted to be a writer, I thought it would be good and maybe useful to read English Language & Literature at university. But after the first year, I started to get seriously ill. I had to come down from Uni. I had developed a cyst on the Cerebellum in my brain that was slowly crushing it.

A long story made short… I had to have emergency neurosurgery which saved my life. The consultant surgeon who operated told me afterwards that I had had about an hour to live.

I recovered and went home to recuperate. But what do I do now? I had done nothing in my life except study. I had met no one, seen nothing, been nowhere. And I had nearly died. I decided to take a small holiday to Greece and have a think.

So, I hitch-hiked to Athens (You could do that, back then, and took a cargo boat to the island of Ios in the Cyclades. I met a German girl there who said I really ought to work on a kibbutz (in Israel) for a while. Her friends had; and had a great time.

So, after a bit of travelling in the Greek islands, I planned to meet up with an English friend in Cyprus (We stayed a while in Cyprus in September, which was the time of year for the island’s Wine festival.[intimate message omitted], where we just carried crates (of full heavy bottles) from here to there. At work, hardly anyone spoke English, but we got away with VERY broken Greek. There was a young guy, working there who would always mess about. He didn’t seem that interested in doing any heavy lifting. One day, he did something unusually stupid and one of the Greek guys who worked with us, shook his head, pointed at the young guy and said, in English, “Small thinker…”

Yes, I thought. That is EXACTLY what he was. I’ve met several in my time.

I went back to Athens and took a flight to Tel Aviv. I succeeded in meeting up with some of the friends of this girl, and together we took a bus to Netanya and on to their “old” kibbutz.

I knew nothing as to what a kibbutz was (it’s a farm) or how you get to work on one. I would be what they called a “volunteer”, which all non-Israelis there were. There were lots of different jobs, I laid irrigation pipes in the cotton fields, picked cotton, picked olives, learnt to drive a tractor (might come in useful, I thought), worked in the kitchen and cleaned out the cowshed (the Refet).

I shared a room with a Honduran guy, Da-veed (to use the Spanish intonation) who slept with a machete under his pillow (I didn’t ask.) He would call me Ga-been in the Spanish way. Notwithstanding the machete, Da-veed was one of the sweetest (and shyest) people you could ever wish to meet. We became good friends.

There were very beautiful (non-Israeli) girls there from all over the world, but I became very friendly with an attractive Israeli girl who seemed not to work on the kibbutz but maybe grew up there because she would come and go as she pleased, it seemed. Moreover, she had a room there. She told me she was in the Army. That was a possibility, I suppose, but I never ever saw her in uniform. (Why didn’t she just say that she worked in the fruit market in Netanya (the nearest town)?

I would have said something moronic such as, “Oh right, cool!”)

To be continued...

[3 photo] I never told you about it, but when I was a kid, I had a big dream of becoming a journalist. I wanted to write articles, cover the news, and raise important topics so that people would read and listen to me. [intimate message omitted], wrote hundreds of essays, sat at night over my notebooks... [intimate message omitted]. Now it seems far away, but sometimes I think, what if that girl who believed that words could change the world still lives inside me? Thank you for being the one with whom I can share even such pieces of myself

After a long stay there I flew back to the UK and decided to take a computer programming course in Brighton. I was walking around Brighton one afternoon and saw an advert in a recruitment agency for a software engineer for a Swedish company developing software for telecommunication. I would never ever call myself one of those, but I applied anyway.

I was called to an interview with Mr Eriksson, whom I took to be the big boss. (I knew nothing.) Ericsson / Eriksson is a very common name in Sweden. Gunnar Eriksson was very interested in what I’d been doing, where I’d travelled to, had I worked there… Pretty much everything except (fortunately, telecommunications).

Ericsson is a multi-national company and he was interested to see if I could work in a multi-national environment. (Of course I could.) We spoke German for some of the interview, again he was testing me to see if I really could.

I was called to a second interview but, after that, I was in. Then my international career began: Dallas, Stockholm, Rome, Melbourne, Paris, Montreal, Madrid. Wonderful places and wonderful people for 28years.

How touching to read your memories. [intimate message omitted]. Such teachers are a real treasure, they don't just teach, they help you to open up and see something special in yourself. In my case, it was an art and foreign literature teacher. She always read each of our papers very carefully, spared no time and effort to write detailed comments, not dry grades, but real reflections, hints, and praise where we did not notice it ourselves. Thanks to her, my love for creativity, for trying to express myself with words, colors, and feelings, has never faded.

I try to answer each of your letters in detail and describe some moments from my life. i hope you will not miss any of them. I will never forget this conversation and thank you for writing with all the details

[2 photo] Your story touches me to the core. How you did not break down after such a terrible illness and surgery, but chose to live life with an open heart, with risk and curiosity. It is inspiring. You put the world together piece by piece, and each piece made you who you are today.

Once I was also left all alone and realized that I couldn't go on like this. I had to change something. And I chose Greece. I didn't make it to Athens, but I saw the mountains and tried the local food. And most of all, I was shocked by the Meteors, these incredible monasteries that seem to float on top of huge rocks. You stand down there and can't believe your eyes, how did people ever build temples there? I also caught the moment when the walls were painted, a stunning action. At that moment, I felt tiny and at the same time infinitely alive. Tell us, please, what happened next? How did your friendship with that mysterious girl from the army develop?

Wow, I love your b/w photos. I always love when you take in black & white.

About the Israeli girl, she was just like any other young girl. Between the ages 18-21, all boys do military training. they are very fit, very very we-trained in armed combat. The girls do 2 years, or did when I was living there. They are also well- trained. Diane was very well trained in unarmed combat. I thought she me might be in special forces or something like that. I was joking about "Kidon" Kidon is a Hebrew word meaning bayonet or something like that. [intimate message omitted]. They know unarmed combat, knife-fighting, and how to kill silently. And they know about weapons.

Diane and I were lovers. She really enjoyed herself when she was with me. When we were messing about, it's possible I said something cheeky and she thought she'd teach me a lesson. She didn't hurt me because if that was her goal, she would have hurt me. Putting me on the floor was the least she could do.

[2 photo] My dear Anna...

I'm writing to you because I can't keep this to myself any longer. Every minute you're not here, I think only of your lips. The way they softly touch mine, at first timidly, almost weightlessly, and then more boldly, deeply, greedily... I imagine you pressed against me, your breath mingling with mine, and I lose my head over that sweet, warm, so-your-very-sweet taste.

I want your kisses until my fingers tremble. I want you to kiss me slowly, exploring every corner of my lips, then my neck, my collarbones... so that your kisses leave marks, like tiny burns, that I'll feel all day. I want you to kiss me so hard, as if you're afraid it won't happen tomorrow, so that I'll choke with desire and whisper your name between moans.

Anna, I'm crazy about your lips. How they can be so tender and yet so powerful. I want them right now, here, in this silence, in this night. I want to drown in you until the whole world disappears.

Are you also thinking about kissing me right now? How much do you want it? 💋

Your Alisa, who can no longer breathe without you.
